title: Care Coordination and Comprehensive Discharge Planning for a MEDICALLY COMPLEX 6-Year-Old Girl with Severe Spastic Quadriplegic Cerebral Palsy (GMFCS V), a Chronic TRACHEOSTOMY on a Home Portable Ventilator, a G-TUBE for Nutrition, an Active SEIZURE DISORDER on Levetiracetam, and Spasticity on Baclofen — Recovering from Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia: The MEDICAL-HOME Model and Interdisciplinary Care Plan, Verified Family CAREGIVER Teach-Back (Trach Suction, G-Tube, Seizure RESCUE, Medications), Confirming Home Equipment + HOME HEALTH + DME + Pharmacy BEFORE Discharge, Medication Reconciliation and a Written EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN, SCHOOL Re-Entry (IEP/504 + Trained School Nurse + Transportation), Caregiver Respite + Sibling Support + Financial/Insurance Navigation (Medicaid Waiver/SSI), Pediatric ADVANCE CARE PLANNING, and a 48-72-Hour Post-Discharge Follow-Up
subtitle: MEDICALLY COMPLEX children (MCC) — children with chronic, severe conditions, technology dependence (trach, ventilator, g-tube, central line), multi-organ involvement, and high health-care use — represent a small fraction of the pediatric population but account for the majority of pediatric hospitalizations, costs, and readmissions. They also are the children whose outcomes most depend on CARE COORDINATION. This case centers on the comprehensive, multidisciplinary discharge planning required to safely transition such a child home after acute illness — distinct from disease-focused pediatric cases (e.g., CF exacerbation case-103, epiglottitis case-111, status asthmaticus case-013) and from elder care coordination cases (medication reconciliation in case-108, hip-fracture surrogate consent case-132). The MEDICAL-HOME model identifies a primary care office (or a designated care-coordination RN) as the central integrator of every specialist (pulmonology, neurology, GI, orthopedics, PT/OT/SLP, palliative care), every service (home health nursing, DME, pharmacy, respite, school nurse, social work, behavioral health), and every transition. A high-quality discharge bundle for MCC must address: (1) CLINICAL READINESS — the acute issue is resolving, vital signs and respiratory status are at her chronic baseline, no new feeding/seizure changes, no new equipment that has not been demonstrated stable at home settings; (2) FAMILY CAREGIVER READINESS — verified TEACH-BACK and RETURN DEMONSTRATION of every essential skill before discharge: TRACH care (humidification, suctioning technique, sterile vs clean per home protocol, emergency trach change with spare same-size + one-size-smaller tubes at bedside, signs of obstruction/decannulation and the response), G-TUBE care (feeds, flushes, medications by tube, dislodgement plan — for a mature stoma cover with sterile gauze and notify provider; replacement of a Foley as a temporizer only if trained and ordered), SEIZURE rescue (recognize, time the seizure, position safely, suction if needed, administer rescue benzodiazepine — e.g., midazolam intranasal or rectal diazepam — when seizure exceeds the protocol time, when to call 911), MEDICATION administration (levetiracetam, baclofen, others — exact doses/times/routes/teach-back), and infection prevention; (3) HOME ENVIRONMENT — equipment (portable ventilator with backup, suction, pulse oximeter, feeding pump, wheelchair, hospital bed, generator/UPS for power outages, communication AAC device), supplies (trach kits, suction catheters, g-tube supplies, formula, medications), and HOME HEALTH NURSING set up via the DME company and home-health agency — confirmed and verified BEFORE discharge, not assumed; (4) MEDICATION RECONCILIATION across hospital → home; (5) WRITTEN CARE PLAN that includes the diagnosis list, baseline status, current medications and schedule, a clear EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN (for trach emergency, seizure, respiratory distress — exactly what to do, when to call 911), the contact list for every specialist and service, the upcoming appointment schedule, and a copy of any advance care directive/POLST; (6) SCHOOL RE-ENTRY — early notification, IEP/504 plan, school nurse trained on her trach/g-tube/seizure rescue and supplied with rescue medications and a written individual emergency action plan, accessible transportation, and a substitute-staff plan; (7) PSYCHOSOCIAL + EQUITY supports — caregiver mental health, RESPITE CARE, SIBLING support, financial/insurance navigation (state Medicaid waiver for medically fragile children, SSI, charitable foundations), housing/transportation/food security; (8) PEDIATRIC ADVANCE CARE PLANNING — appropriate (and protective) in medically complex children with serious chronic illness; involves the family in conversations about goals, code status, and a POLST/MOLST as desired, and is revisited as conditions change; (9) FOLLOW-UP — a phone call or visit within 48-72 hours of discharge catches missed medications and unsafe-discharge issues and prevents readmission. KEY PRINCIPLES: PARTNER with the family — they are the experts on this child; communicate using STRUCTURED handoffs (SBAR, written care plan); verify rather than assume; integrate palliative care EARLY as a layer of comfort and decision support (not 'giving up'); and recognize that the goal is not just survival but family-defined quality of life. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: discharging before equipment/home-health are confirmed; skipping teach-back because the family 'has done this before'; sending the child to school without notifying the school nurse and providing a written emergency action plan; relying on verbal handoffs in place of a written care plan; treating care coordination as optional; avoiding advance care planning because the child is young (pediatric ACP is appropriate and protective). NURSING ROLE: confirm clinical and family readiness with verified teach-back; confirm home equipment + home health + DME + pharmacy IN PLACE before discharge; reconcile medications and finalize a written care plan with an explicit emergency action plan and contact list; coordinate school re-entry with the school nurse trained and supplied; arrange psychosocial, respite, sibling, and financial supports; integrate palliative care and offer pediatric advance care planning; and schedule a 48-72-hour follow-up — anchoring every step in the medical home and respecting the family as expert.

patient.name: A. Mireles
patient.code: Full code at this time per family; medically complex chronic patient on a home portable ventilator via tracheostomy + g-tube; goal = safe, coordinated discharge from hospital to home and school with verified family caregiver readiness, confirmed home equipment + home health + DME + pharmacy, a written comprehensive care plan with an explicit emergency action plan, school re-entry coordination, psychosocial/respite/sibling/financial supports, palliative care + pediatric advance care planning integration, and a 48-72-hour follow-up
patient.admitDate: Former 26-week preterm with severe spastic quadriplegic cerebral palsy from periventricular leukomalacia (GMFCS V — non-ambulatory, total care, nonverbal — communicates via eye gaze and an AAC device); chronic respiratory failure on a HOME PORTABLE VENTILATOR via tracheostomy; G-TUBE for nutrition; SEIZURE DISORDER on levetiracetam (rescue: intranasal midazolam per plan); SPASTICITY on oral baclofen; followed by pulmonology, neurology, GI, orthopedics, PT/OT/SLP, and palliative care. Admitted 5 days ago for hospital-acquired pneumonia — resolving, back to chronic baseline. Mother is the primary caregiver (very experienced); father works full time; two siblings ages 4 and 9. Today's nursing focus: COMPREHENSIVE DISCHARGE PLANNING + CARE COORDINATION to safely transition her home and back to school, anchored in the medical-home model.
patient.room: Pediatric medical-surgical unit; interdisciplinary team (pediatric hospitalist, pediatric pulmonology, neurology, GI, palliative care, complex-care RN/care coordinator, respiratory therapy, child-life, social work, dietitian, PT/OT/SLP, school-liaison nurse, DME company, home-health agency, primary care office); family meeting scheduled

vitals.temp: 37.0°C; at chronic baseline; afebrile x 48 hours; pneumonia resolving; secretions back to baseline; tolerating g-tube feeds at home rate; no new seizure activity; cleared by hospitalist + pulm + neuro + GI for discharge with comprehensive coordination

37.0°C; mother demonstrates trach suctioning, emergency trach change (spare same-size + one-size-smaller at bedside), g-tube care/flushes/meds by tube, seizure recognition + intranasal midazolam rescue + when-to-call-911, and medication administration with correct doses/times; teach-back verified for each skill before discharge

vitals.time: Home equipment + home health + DME + pharmacy CONFIRMED
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; portable home ventilator + backup + suction + pulse oximeter + feeding pump + wheelchair + hospital bed + AAC device + generator/UPS — confirmed in the home and tested; supplies stocked (trach kits, suction catheters, g-tube + formula, medications); HOME HEALTH NURSING start-of-care visit scheduled within 24 hours; primary care follow-up in 1 week; specialists scheduled

vitals.time: Medication reconciliation + written care plan + EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; medication reconciliation completed across hospital → home; written comprehensive care plan finalized — diagnoses + baseline + current medications + EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN (trach obstruction/decannulation, seizure, respiratory distress — exact steps + when to call 911) + contact list for every specialist/service + appointment schedule + copy of POLST shared per family wishes

37.0°C; school-liaison nurse engaged — IEP/504 in place; school nurse trained on trach care, g-tube use, seizure rescue (midazolam intranasal) and supplied; written individual emergency action plan provided to school; transportation arranged; siblings receive child-life support; caregiver respite and Medicaid-waiver/SSI navigation arranged with social work; palliative-care team integrated

37.0°C; care coordinator places a 48-72-hour follow-up call — catches a missing levetiracetam refill (corrected same day by the home-health RN and pharmacy), confirms vent + g-tube + feeds + seizure plan are working, addresses caregiver questions, and prevents a return visit; primary care/medical-home reinforced as central integrator

labs.n: PRESENTATION + the medically complex child and the medical-home model
labs.v: 6 y/o A. Mireles, former 26-week preterm with severe spastic quadriplegic CEREBRAL PALSY (GMFCS V — non-ambulatory, total care, nonverbal/AAC), chronic respiratory failure on a HOME PORTABLE VENTILATOR via tracheostomy, G-TUBE for nutrition, SEIZURE DISORDER on levetiracetam (rescue midazolam per plan), spasticity on baclofen, followed by pulmonology, neurology, GI, orthopedics, PT/OT/SLP, and palliative care. Admitted 5 days ago for hospital-acquired pneumonia — now resolving, at chronic baseline, cleared for discharge. The nursing task is COORDINATED DISCHARGE PLANNING: medically complex children have the highest readmission risk and benefit most from structured care coordination. The MEDICAL-HOME model designates a central integrator (primary care office + complex-care coordinator RN) who connects every specialist, service, and transition — distinct from disease-focused pediatric cases (CF 103, epiglottitis 111, status asthmaticus 013) and from elder care coordination cases (108, 132). MOM is the primary caregiver (very experienced); father works full time; two siblings ages 4 and 9. Today's flow: verify clinical + family readiness → confirm home equipment + home health + DME + pharmacy → reconcile medications + finalize a written care plan + emergency action plan → coordinate school re-entry → address psychosocial/financial/respite/sibling supports + palliative + ACP → schedule 48-72-hour follow-up — anchored in the medical home.
labs.r: Medically complex children (technology-dependent, multi-organ) drive most pediatric readmissions and benefit most from care coordination. MEDICAL-HOME model = a central integrator (PCP + complex-care coordinator RN) connecting every specialist/service/transition. RN role: verify clinical + family readiness, confirm home setup, reconcile meds + written plan + emergency action plan, coordinate school + psychosocial/financial/respite/ACP + 48-72h follow-up. Family = experts on their child; partner, don't dictate.

labs.n: Family caregiver TEACH-BACK on essential skills (trach, g-tube, seizure rescue, meds)
labs.v: The single most important safety step before discharge is verified TEACH-BACK and RETURN DEMONSTRATION by the family caregiver(s) of every essential skill — even (especially) for experienced caregivers, because techniques and equipment change. TRACH care: humidification, suctioning technique with correct catheter size and depth and limited duration with hyperoxygenation pre/post per home protocol, sterile vs clean technique per home plan, emergency TRACH CHANGE with a SPARE same-size tube AND a one-size-smaller tube and obturator at the bedside at all times, recognizing OBSTRUCTION (rising peak pressures on the vent, falling SpO2, distress) and DECANNULATION (immediate distress, displaced tube) and the response (suction or replace), keeping the stoma clean, and infection signs to report. G-TUBE care: feeds at home rate via pump, flushes before/after feeds and medications, MEDICATIONS by tube (liquid where possible; never crush extended-release/enteric-coated; flush between), dislodgement plan (mature stoma: cover with sterile gauze and notify provider; only attempt Foley placement as a temporizer if trained and ordered — never blindly reinsert a fresh PEG; see case-140), peristomal skin care. SEIZURE rescue: recognize, time the seizure, position safely, suction the trach if needed, administer the RESCUE benzodiazepine per the action plan (e.g., intranasal midazolam or rectal diazepam) at the protocol time, when to call 911 (status epilepticus, respiratory compromise beyond baseline, prolonged postictal). MEDICATIONS: exact doses, times, and routes; refill management. Teach-back is VERIFIED for each skill before discharge — not assumed.
labs.r: TEACH-BACK + return demonstration on every essential skill is non-negotiable. TRACH: humidification, suction (correct catheter/depth/duration + hyperoxygenate), spare same-size + one-size-smaller tubes + obturator at bedside, emergency change for obstruction/decannulation, infection signs. G-TUBE: feeds/flushes, meds by tube (liquids preferred; never crush ER/EC; flush between), dislodgement plan (mature stoma cover + notify; Foley only if trained/ordered; never blindly reinsert fresh PEG), skin care. SEIZURE rescue: recognize + time + position safely + suction trach + intranasal midazolam (or rectal diazepam) per plan + when to call 911. MEDS: exact doses/times/routes/refills. RN role: verify teach-back, don't assume.

labs.n: Home equipment + medication reconciliation + written care plan + emergency action plan + school re-entry
labs.v: HOME ENVIRONMENT must be CONFIRMED in place before discharge, not assumed: portable HOME VENTILATOR with backup + circuit/filters + tubing, SUCTION machine + spare, PULSE OXIMETER, FEEDING PUMP, WHEELCHAIR + positioning, hospital BED, AAC communication device, and a GENERATOR or UPS for power outages (high-yield for ventilator-dependent children). Supplies stocked: trach kits, suction catheters, g-tube supplies, formula, medications. HOME HEALTH NURSING is set up via the DME company and home-health agency with a start-of-care visit ideally within 24 hours, and the family knows whom to call after hours. MEDICATION RECONCILIATION reconciles hospital → home medications (levetiracetam, baclofen, etc.) and is documented; the pharmacy delivery is confirmed for the day of discharge. A WRITTEN COMPREHENSIVE CARE PLAN is finalized and given to the family (and shared with the medical home, specialists, and school): diagnoses + baseline, current medications and schedule, an explicit EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN (trach obstruction/decannulation, seizure, respiratory distress — exact stepwise actions + when to call 911), contact list for every specialist/service/24-hour line, the appointment schedule, and a copy of any advance care directive/POLST. SCHOOL RE-ENTRY is coordinated early: the school nurse is notified, an IEP/504 plan is in place, the school nurse is trained on her trach care, g-tube use, and seizure rescue (with intranasal midazolam supplied) and given a written individual emergency action plan, transportation is arranged, and a substitute-staff plan exists. PSYCHOSOCIAL: caregiver mental health, RESPITE care (in-home or facility), SIBLING support (child-life involvement), financial/insurance navigation (Medicaid waiver for medically fragile children, SSI, charitable foundations, mileage/parking, lodging if travel), housing/food/transportation security, and culturally appropriate care. PALLIATIVE CARE is integrated EARLY as a layer of comfort and decision support (not 'giving up'); pediatric ADVANCE CARE PLANNING is appropriate and protective and involves the family in goal/code-status/POLST conversations, revisited as the disease/condition evolves.
labs.r: HOME ENV: vent + backup + suction + pulse ox + feeding pump + wheelchair + bed + AAC + GENERATOR/UPS; supplies stocked. HOME HEALTH set up via DME + agency; SOC visit within 24h. MED REC reconciled hospital→home + pharmacy delivery confirmed. WRITTEN CARE PLAN: dx/baseline + meds + EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN (trach emergency/seizure/resp distress + when to 911) + contact list + appointments + advance directive/POLST. SCHOOL RE-ENTRY: IEP/504 + trained + supplied school nurse w/ written individual emergency action plan + transportation + substitute plan. PSYCHOSOCIAL: caregiver MH + RESPITE + SIBLING support + financial (Medicaid waiver/SSI/foundations) + housing/transport/food + culture. PALLIATIVE early + pediatric ACP/POLST. RN role: confirm and coordinate, don't assume.

labs.v: A 48-72-HOUR POST-DISCHARGE FOLLOW-UP (phone call or visit) is one of the most effective single interventions to prevent pediatric readmissions in medically complex children — it catches missing prescriptions/refills, equipment gaps, family questions, and emerging concerns before they become emergencies. Here it caught a missing levetiracetam refill (resolved same day by home health + pharmacy). The MEDICAL HOME (primary care office + complex-care coordinator) remains the central integrator across hospitalizations, specialist visits, and school. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the team did NOT discharge before home equipment/home health/DME/pharmacy were confirmed; did NOT skip teach-back/return demonstration because the family is experienced; did NOT send the child to school without notifying the school nurse and providing a written individual emergency action plan; did NOT rely on verbal handoffs instead of a written care plan; did NOT treat care coordination as optional; did NOT avoid pediatric advance care planning because she is young; and did NOT crush extended-release medications for the g-tube or instill saline routinely down the trach during suctioning. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: clinical + family caregiver readiness verified by teach-back; home environment + equipment + supplies + home health + DME + pharmacy CONFIRMED before discharge; medication reconciliation; written care plan + explicit EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN + contact list + appointment schedule; coordinated school re-entry with a trained school nurse; comprehensive psychosocial supports + respite + sibling support + financial navigation; palliative care + pediatric ACP integrated; a 48-72-hour follow-up tied to the medical home; and a partnership stance that respects the family as expert. EXPECTED outcome: she transitions home and back to school safely, no preventable readmission, a quality of life her family chose, and a sustainable plan for the long road ahead.
labs.r: 48-72h post-discharge follow-up = highest-yield readmission-prevention step (caught a missed levetiracetam refill here). Negatives confirm correct practice (didn't discharge before home/DME/HH confirmed; didn't skip teach-back; didn't send to school without nurse training + emergency plan; didn't rely on verbal handoffs; didn't skip ACP because she's young; didn't crush ER for g-tube or routine-saline the trach). Protective: clinical + family readiness verified + home setup confirmed + med rec + written care plan + EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN + contact list + appointment schedule + school re-entry + psychosocial/respite/sibling/financial + palliative + pediatric ACP + 48-72h follow-up + medical home. RN role: coordinate, verify, partner, follow up.

nursesNote: Pediatric med-surg — 6 y/o, GMFCS V CP from PVL (former 26-wk preterm), nonverbal/AAC + total care; chronic respiratory failure on HOME PORTABLE VENT via TRACHEOSTOMY; G-TUBE for nutrition; seizure d/o on levetiracetam (rescue intranasal midazolam per plan); spasticity on oral baclofen; followed by pulm/neuro/GI/ortho/PT-OT-SLP/palliative. Admitted 5 d for HAP — resolving, back to chronic baseline, afebrile x 48h, secretions baseline, tolerating g-tube feeds at home rate, no new seizures. Cleared for discharge w/ comprehensive coordination. MEDICAL-HOME model anchors the plan. CHECKLIST: (1) Family TEACH-BACK + return demonstration verified for each essential skill — TRACH suction (correct catheter/depth/duration + hyperoxygenate), spare same-size + one-size-smaller tubes + obturator at bedside, emergency trach change for obstruction/decannulation, infection signs; G-TUBE feeds/flushes + meds by tube (liquids preferred, never crush ER/EC, flush between), dislodgement plan (mature stoma cover + notify; Foley only if trained/ordered; never blindly reinsert); SEIZURE rescue (recognize + time + position safely + suction trach + intranasal midazolam per plan + when to 911); MEDS doses/times/routes. (2) HOME equipment confirmed: portable vent + backup, suction, pulse ox, feeding pump, wheelchair, hospital bed, AAC device, GENERATOR/UPS; trach/g-tube supplies + formula + meds stocked; HOME HEALTH SOC within 24h; PCP follow-up 1 wk; specialists scheduled. (3) MED REC reconciled hospital→home + pharmacy delivery confirmed for the day. (4) WRITTEN CARE PLAN finalized: diagnoses + baseline + current meds + EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN (trach obstruction/decannulation, seizure, respiratory distress — exact steps + when to 911) + contact list (every specialist/service/24-h line) + appointment schedule + POLST per family wishes; shared with medical home + specialists + school. (5) SCHOOL RE-ENTRY: IEP/504 in place; school nurse trained on trach/g-tube/seizure rescue + supplied with intranasal midazolam + a written INDIVIDUAL emergency action plan; transportation arranged; substitute-staff plan. (6) PSYCHOSOCIAL: caregiver MH + RESPITE + SIBLING support (child-life) + Medicaid-waiver/SSI/foundations via SW; palliative integrated early as comfort/decision support; pediatric ACP revisited as conditions change. (7) 48-72h FOLLOW-UP phone call placed — caught a missed levetiracetam refill (resolved same day by home health + pharmacy), confirmed vent/feeds/seizure plan working. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT discharge before equipment/home-health/DME/pharmacy confirmed, did NOT skip teach-back because family is experienced, did NOT send to school without nurse training + written individual emergency plan, did NOT use verbal-only handoffs (written care plan provided), did NOT skip pediatric ACP because she's young, did NOT crush ER for g-tube or routine-saline the trach. Outcome: safe discharge to home + school; no preventable readmission; sustainable plan; family as expert partner.
providerOrders: DISCHARGE PLANNING: comprehensive interdisciplinary discharge for a medically complex pediatric patient; anchor in the medical-home model with the primary care office and a complex-care coordinator RN as central integrators
CLINICAL READINESS: confirm return to chronic baseline (vital signs, respiratory status, feeds, seizures); pneumonia resolved; cleared by hospitalist, pulmonology, neurology, and GI

FAMILY CAREGIVER EDUCATION: verified teach-back and return demonstration of every essential skill before discharge — tracheostomy care and suctioning, emergency trach change with a spare same-size and one-size-smaller tube and obturator at the bedside, g-tube feeds/flushes and medication administration through the tube (no crushing of extended-release/enteric-coated meds; flush between meds; never blindly reinsert a freshly dislodged g-tube), seizure rescue with intranasal midazolam per the action plan, and infection prevention

HOME EQUIPMENT/SUPPLIES: confirm in-home placement and function of portable ventilator with backup, suction, pulse oximeter, feeding pump, wheelchair/positioning, hospital bed, AAC communication device, and a generator/UPS for power outages; trach kits, suction catheters, g-tube and formula supplies, and medications stocked
HOME HEALTH NURSING: start-of-care visit within 24 hours via the home-health agency and DME company; 24/7 contact line provided
MEDICATION RECONCILIATION + PHARMACY: reconcile every medication hospital → home; confirm pharmacy delivery on the day of discharge; ensure refills for the next 30 days

WRITTEN COMPREHENSIVE CARE PLAN: diagnoses + baseline, current medications and schedule, explicit EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN (tracheostomy obstruction/decannulation, seizure, respiratory distress — stepwise actions and when to call 911), contact list for every specialist and service with 24-hour numbers, appointment schedule, and a copy of any advance care directive/POLST

SCHOOL RE-ENTRY: notify the school nurse and complete IEP/504 plan; train the school nurse on tracheostomy care, g-tube use, and seizure rescue (with intranasal midazolam supplied); provide a written individual emergency action plan; arrange accessible transportation and a substitute-staff plan

PSYCHOSOCIAL/EQUITY: arrange caregiver respite, sibling support (child-life), Medicaid waiver for medically fragile children/SSI/foundation navigation, and culturally appropriate support; integrate palliative care early as a comfort and decision-support layer
PEDIATRIC ADVANCE CARE PLANNING: offer family conversations about goals, code status, and POLST/MOLST as appropriate; revisit as the disease/condition evolves

FOLLOW-UP: place a 48-72-hour post-discharge phone call to verify medications, equipment, feeding, seizures, and family questions; primary care follow-up within 1 week; specialist follow-ups per schedule

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this medically complex discharge?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: A medically complex child (severe CP, tracheostomy on a home portable vent, g-tube, seizure disorder) being discharged after acute illness — care coordination is the central nursing task
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — medically complex children are most at risk of unsafe discharge and readmission; coordination is essential. RN role: lead the coordination.

opts.text: Multiple specialists (pulmonology, neurology, GI, orthopedics, PT/OT/SLP, palliative) and services (home health, DME, pharmacy, school, social work) must be integrated through a medical-home model
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the medical-home model centralizes the integrator and reduces handoff errors. RN role: anchor in the medical home.

opts.text: Family caregiver readiness must be verified by TEACH-BACK and return demonstration on every essential skill (trach suctioning, emergency trach change, g-tube, seizure rescue, medications)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — verified teach-back is the safety floor for technology-dependent care at home. RN role: verify, don't assume.

opts.text: Home equipment, supplies, home health nursing, DME, and pharmacy delivery must be CONFIRMED in place before discharge — and a generator/UPS is critical for a ventilator-dependent child
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — assumed-but-not-confirmed home setup is a common, dangerous failure point. RN role: confirm in place.

opts.text: A written comprehensive care plan with an explicit emergency action plan, contact list, and appointment schedule — plus medication reconciliation — must travel with the family and be shared with the medical home, specialists, and school
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — written plans replace fragile verbal handoffs. RN role: written + shared.

opts.text: School re-entry needs IEP/504, a trained and supplied school nurse, a written individual emergency action plan, and accessible transportation — early in the process, not at the school door
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — school inclusion + safety depend on prepared, trained, supplied school staff. RN role: school coordination.

opts.text: Caregiver respite, sibling support, financial/insurance navigation, palliative care integration, and pediatric advance care planning are part of comprehensive coordination — and a 48-72-hour follow-up is high-yield for preventing readmission
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — psychosocial/equity and proactive follow-up are core, not optional. RN role: full coordination + follow-up.

opts.text: Discharge today as soon as her pneumonia is resolved, without confirming home equipment, home health, or family training
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Discharging before equipment/home health/family training are in place is the classic unsafe-discharge failure for technology-dependent children.

opts.text: Send her back to her general classroom without notifying the school nurse since the family will manage her trach themselves
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The school nurse must be notified and trained, supplied, and given a written individual emergency action plan; inclusion does not work without a prepared school team.

opts.text: Medically complex children account for a small fraction of children but the majority of pediatric hospitalizations and readmissions; structured care coordination reduces readmissions and improves outcomes
opts.rat: CORRECT — the epidemiology and rationale for coordination. Foundation for prioritizing the work.

opts.text: The medical-home model designates a primary-care-anchored coordinator (often a complex-care RN) as the central integrator across specialists, services, and transitions
opts.rat: CORRECT — the operational model. Foundation for who owns the plan.

opts.text: Family caregivers are core team members and the experts on their child; structured teach-back partners with the family rather than testing them
opts.rat: CORRECT — partnership principle. Foundation for verified-yet-respectful caregiver education.

opts.text: Early palliative care integration in serious chronic pediatric conditions is appropriate and beneficial — it adds comfort and decision support without 'giving up'
opts.rat: CORRECT — early palliative principle. Foundation for comprehensive care.

opts.text: School inclusion of a medically complex child requires an IEP/504 plan, a trained and supplied school nurse, a written individual emergency action plan, and accessible transportation
opts.rat: CORRECT — school re-entry principle. Foundation for safe school participation.

opts.text: A 48-72-hour post-discharge follow-up call/visit catches missing prescriptions, equipment gaps, and emerging concerns and is one of the highest-yield single interventions to prevent readmission
opts.rat: CORRECT — follow-up principle. Foundation for closing the loop.

opts.text: Care coordination is optional — if the family is engaged, the system will self-organize
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Even highly engaged families cannot integrate ten specialties, three services, a school, and equipment alone; coordination is essential and is a core nursing responsibility.

opts.text: Pediatric advance care planning should be avoided in a young child because she is too young to discuss serious illness
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Pediatric ACP is appropriate and protective in medically complex children with serious chronic illness; it involves the family and is revisited as the condition evolves.

opts.text: Verify clinical readiness AND family caregiver readiness with teach-back/return demonstration on every essential skill (trach, emergency trach change, g-tube, seizure rescue, medications)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Without verified clinical and caregiver readiness, nothing else is safe. RN role: verify readiness.

opts.text: Confirm home equipment + supplies + home health nursing + DME + pharmacy delivery in place BEFORE discharge (with generator/UPS for vent-dependence)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Confirmed (not assumed) home setup is the operational foundation of safe technology-dependent care. RN role: confirm in place.

opts.text: Reconcile medications, finalize a written comprehensive care plan with an explicit emergency action plan + contact list + appointment schedule, and share with the medical home/specialists/school
opts.rat: #3 — Replaces fragile verbal handoffs with a portable written plan. RN role: written + shared plan.

opts.text: Coordinate school re-entry — IEP/504, train and supply the school nurse, written individual emergency action plan, transportation, substitute-staff plan
opts.rat: #4 — Enables safe school inclusion and avoids re-injury at re-entry. RN role: school coordination.

opts.text: Address psychosocial/financial supports, respite, sibling support, palliative care, and pediatric ACP; schedule a 48-72-hour follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Sustains family + child over the long road and prevents readmission. RN role: psychosocial + follow-up.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this discharge.

opts.text: Verify family caregiver teach-back/return demonstration on trach suctioning, emergency trach change with a spare same-size and one-size-smaller tube at the bedside, g-tube, seizure rescue with intranasal midazolam, and medications
opts.rat: Verified caregiver readiness is the safety floor for technology-dependent home care. RN role: teach-back.

opts.text: Confirm home equipment (vent + backup, suction, pulse ox, feeding pump, wheelchair, hospital bed, AAC, generator/UPS), supplies, home health nursing start-of-care, DME, and pharmacy delivery — IN PLACE before discharge
opts.rat: Confirmed home setup prevents unsafe discharge. RN role: confirm in place.

opts.text: Reconcile medications and finalize a written comprehensive care plan with diagnoses, current meds, an EMERGENCY ACTION PLAN, contact list, and appointment schedule — shared with the medical home, specialists, and school
opts.rat: Written plan + emergency action plan replace fragile verbal handoffs. RN role: written + shared.

opts.text: Coordinate school re-entry early — IEP/504, trained and supplied school nurse, written individual emergency action plan, transportation, substitute-staff plan
opts.rat: Safe school inclusion depends on a prepared school team. RN role: school coordination.

opts.text: Arrange caregiver respite, sibling support, financial/insurance navigation (Medicaid waiver for medically fragile children/SSI/foundations), integrate palliative care, and offer pediatric advance care planning
opts.rat: Psychosocial/equity supports and ACP are part of comprehensive coordination. RN role: psychosocial + ACP.

opts.text: Schedule a 48-72-hour post-discharge follow-up call/visit with the care coordinator and the home-health team
opts.rat: Highest-yield single intervention to prevent readmission. RN role: closing the loop.

opts.text: Discharge today as soon as her pneumonia resolves, without confirming home equipment, home health, or family training
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — unsafe discharge for a technology-dependent child; readmission risk is high.

opts.text: Skip teach-back because the family is experienced and has done this for years
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — equipment and techniques change; verified teach-back is non-negotiable even for experienced caregivers.

opts.text: Send her back to her classroom without notifying or training the school nurse and without providing a written individual emergency action plan
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — safe school inclusion requires a prepared, trained, supplied school nurse and a written plan.

opts.text: Avoid pediatric advance care planning because she is young, and crush extended-release medications to give down the g-tube to make it easier
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — ACP is appropriate and protective in serious chronic pediatric illness, and crushing ER/EC meds destroys the delivery mechanism (case-140).

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm clinical readiness and conduct teach-back/return demonstration of every essential family caregiver skill
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Verified readiness anchors every subsequent step. RN role: readiness.

opts.text: Confirm home equipment, supplies, home health nursing start-of-care, DME, and pharmacy delivery in place before discharge
opts.rat: #2 — Second. The operational foundation of safe technology-dependent care. RN role: confirm in place.

opts.text: Reconcile medications and finalize the written care plan with an explicit emergency action plan, contact list, and appointment schedule — shared with the medical home, specialists, and school
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Replaces verbal handoffs with a portable, shareable plan. RN role: written + shared.

opts.text: Coordinate school re-entry (IEP/504, train and supply the school nurse, written individual emergency action plan, transportation)
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Safe school inclusion through a prepared school team. RN role: school coordination.

opts.text: Arrange psychosocial/financial supports, respite, sibling support, palliative + pediatric ACP, and schedule a 48-72-hour follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Closing/ongoing. Sustains family + child and closes the loop. RN role: psychosocial + follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action/outcome, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The team verified clinical readiness, conducted teach-back on every essential skill, and confirmed home equipment + home health + DME + pharmacy in place before discharge
opts.rat: The safety floor for technology-dependent home care. Model practice.

opts.text: The team finalized and shared a written comprehensive care plan with an explicit emergency action plan, contact list, appointment schedule, and POLST per family wishes — with the medical home, specialists, and school
opts.rat: Written + shared replaces fragile verbal handoffs. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: The school nurse was trained on tracheostomy care, g-tube use, and seizure rescue (with intranasal midazolam supplied) and given a written individual emergency action plan, and transportation was arranged
opts.rat: Prepared, supplied school team enables safe inclusion. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Caregiver respite, sibling support, Medicaid-waiver/SSI navigation, palliative care integration, and pediatric advance care planning were arranged
opts.rat: Comprehensive psychosocial and equity supports + ACP. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: A 48-72-hour post-discharge call by the care coordinator caught a missing levetiracetam refill and prevented a readmission
opts.rat: High-yield follow-up closing the loop. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative — she was discharged before home equipment/home health/DME/pharmacy were confirmed, and she returned within 24 hours for an unsafe-discharge readmission
opts.rat: ⚠️ Discharging before the home setup is confirmed is the classic unsafe-discharge failure for technology-dependent children.

opts.text: Alternative — the team skipped teach-back because the family is experienced; an unrecognized trach issue led to a mucus plug at home
opts.rat: ⚠️ Verified teach-back is non-negotiable; experience does not replace it.

opts.text: Alternative — she returned to school without notifying or training the school nurse and without a written individual emergency action plan; a seizure was mishandled
opts.rat: ⚠️ Safe school inclusion requires a prepared school team and a written plan.

opts.text: Alternative — pediatric advance care planning was skipped because she is young, and extended-release medications were crushed and given down the g-tube
opts.rat: ⚠️ Pediatric ACP is appropriate in serious chronic pediatric illness, and crushing ER/EC medications destroys the delivery mechanism (case-140).

opts.text: Alternative — care coordination was treated as optional because the family is engaged; specialists, school, equipment, and follow-up were not integrated and the child was readmitted within 30 days
opts.rat: ⚠️ Even engaged families cannot integrate the full system; coordination is essential and a core nursing responsibility.
